items.title: Europe: the world's fastest-warming continent
items.summary: The latest heat wave sweeping across Europe is a stark reminder that it is the world's fastest-warming continent, stretching into an Arctic that is heating at an even greater pace.

items.title: India monsoon sweeps north but brings less rain than usual
items.summary: Long-awaited monsoon rains arrived in India's financial capital, Mumbai, on Tuesday, cooling weeks of blazing heat despite persistent fears of water shortages, with total rainfall so far staying below the long-term avera…

items.title: Happy Asteroid Day! Prize-winning plan focuses on space infrastructure
items.summary: For decades, astronomers and policymakers have been working on plans to protect our planet from killer asteroids. But now there's a new realm to protect: the thousands of satellites we're putting in orbit.

items.title: Sawdust, cellulose binders and beeswax combine into eco-friendly foam

items.title: How a heat dome is formed and why experts blame one for Europe's baking temperatures
items.summary: Europe is sizzling under an early heat wave this week, with millions of people experiencing extremely high temperatures, and experts say a phenomenon known as a heat dome is to blame.

items.title: Yellow mealworms mapped anatomically for the first time

items.title: Pathway to high-fidelity quantum computing identified
items.summary: Researchers from the University of Sydney, working with IBM, have identified and quantified important factors limiting the performance of quantum computers and demonstrated ways to overcome their impact.

items.title: Attitudes, not personality, may drive deepfake pornography creation
items.summary: New research from Edith Cowan University (ECU) suggests attitudes, particularly those that excuse harmful behavior, may be a stronger predictor of willingness to create deepfake pornography than personality traits. The f…

items.title: Although woodland salamanders have looked the same for millions of years, their physiology has evolved rapidly
items.summary: For her doctoral dissertation, Yale's Nathalie Alomar decided to study a small amphibian that appeared to have eluded the forces of evolution. She found that there is more to its evolution than meets the eye.

items.title: The ‘little brain’ may give the aging mind a big boost
items.summary: Most known for its role in movement, the cerebellum could compensate for flagging mental functions elsewhere in the brain.

items.title: 500-million-year fossil record reveals corals' symbiotic advantage shifted with changing environments

items.title: Looking at AI startups to predict which jobs AI will affect

items.title: New method helps online ads reach overlooked groups
items.summary: Online advertisers and government agencies use algorithmic tools to tailor and target their campaigns to reach as many people as possible.

items.title: Espresso 'pucks' stop behaving predictably above certain pressures
items.summary: When a physics student asked baristas at the Warsaw Coffee Conference what their biggest question for scientists was, the baristas said they wanted to know how to stop channeling during brewing.

items.title: Airflow mystery solved: Heavy rain reshaped airflow through miles of underground tunnels
items.summary: Successfully operating in a deep underground space requires mitigating two factors: air and water.

items.title: Even ‘safe’ air pollution levels may affect heart health
items.summary: An imaging study found early signs of coronary artery disease in people in Canada breathing air that regulators consider clean.
